name: Zod
what_it_does: TypeScript-first schema declaration and validation library that parses unknown data into typed values and infers static TypeScript types from schemas.
use_cases: Validate and parse untrusted JSON payloads received by an agent from external APIs using z.parse() with thrown errors on failure
Extract TypeScript types from schemas via z.infer<typeof schema> to keep runtime validation and compile-time types in sync
Use safeParse() to validate agent inputs without throwing, returning a discriminated union of success/error for structured error handling
Build reusable nested schemas with z.object(), z.array(), z.union(), and z.discriminatedUnion() to model complex data contracts
Transform and coerce data at parse time using .transform() and .refine() to normalize agent inputs before processing
not_for: Runtime environments without TypeScript tooling where the type inference features provide no value
Async validation workflows with complex server-side checks — yup's native async .validate() is more ergonomic for that
Extremely bundle-size-constrained environments — Zod is moderately large; consider Valibot for tree-shakeable alternatives
best_when: Building TypeScript applications where a single schema definition should serve as both the runtime validator and the static type source of truth.
avoid_when: You need async field-level validation against external sources (e.g., database uniqueness checks) as a first-class part of the schema definition.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: ZodError includes a structured issues array with path, code, and message per field — highly machine-readable and suitable for agent error reporting.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Pure parsing function — calling parse() or safeParse() on the same input always returns the same result with no side effects.
known_agent_gotchas: z.parse() throws a ZodError on failure — agents using it in async contexts must wrap in try/catch or switch to safeParse()
Zod strips unknown keys by default with z.object() — use .passthrough() or .strict() to change this behavior, or agent output may silently lose fields
z.infer<> only works at compile time — agents running in plain JavaScript get no type inference benefit and must rely solely on runtime behavior
.optional() makes a field optional (may be undefined) but does NOT strip it from the output; .nullish() accepts both null and undefined
Large nested schemas with many .refine() calls can be slow — agents validating high-throughput streams should benchmark Zod's parse overhead

security_score_components.security_notes: Pure validation library with no network access or secret storage — security posture depends entirely on how the caller handles ZodError details in logs.
